publications.editedComment: Three editable details are on the table: salon name, booking/contact link, and reply contact. Each field saves only on its change event, trimming the values and restoring `Hair by Superkate` when the salon name is blank. A deep watcher mirrors settings that hydrate later from the Superkate store, so local inputs do not remain stuck on their initial values. The receipt preview is computed entirely from those three fields plus fixed sample appointment math and the non-configurable closing line. No receipt is sent or client record changed here. The clue is too clean to miss: local contact settings in, live sample text out, one store update at the boundary.

Treat this as a three-variable receipt prompt. The Component initializes salon name, booking link, and reply contact from `superkate.settings`, watches the settings object for later hydration, and writes trimmed values back through `updateSettings` whenever an input changes. A computed preview assembles greeting, sample date, fixed price calculation, the three editable contact lines, and the required `Superkate loves you!` sign-off. Empty salon input is normalized to `Hair by Superkate`; the other two may remain blank. This is configuration and preview only—no appointment, invoice, or message is persisted from this surface.

This signal changes destination with identity. Guests see the person icon and route to `/login`; logged-in users see `user-avatar` and route to `/dashboard`. The label is derived from the user's role in title case, with an unread-message suffix when any Chat-store message names the active user as recipient. The same computed label feeds both title and aria-label and is exposed to a parent. A small red badge marks unread state but does not display a count. No login, message fetch, or read mutation occurs here. The fox reports the current network condition and points toward the proper node.

The little vane points one of two ways: `/login` for a guest, `/dashboard` for an authenticated user. Logged-in display uses the shared avatar Component; guest display uses the person icon. The label comes from the account role, converted from values such as ADMIN to Admin, and adds `(unread messages)` when the Chat store contains a message addressed to the current user. That unread condition also raises a dot on the avatar. The label is exposed for Smartbar use. This Component observes existing user and chat state—it does not authenticate anyone or clear a message.

The mechanism reads `stayLoggedIn` from the User store's local-storage helper on mount. Missing data defaults to true; otherwise only the literal string `true` enables persistence. It immediately synchronizes that choice through `setStayLoggedIn`, then marks initialization complete so the watcher does not mistake hydration for a user change. Later checkbox changes call the same store method and show a matching lock or session-only toast for 2.5 seconds. The timer is not retained or cancelled on unmount. This control selects session persistence policy; it does not perform login, logout, token refresh, or credential storage by itself.

A shiny binary choice, and the store keeps it. On mount the Component retrieves `stayLoggedIn`; absence becomes true, while a saved value must equal the string `true`. That initial state is passed to `userStore.setStayLoggedIn` before the watcher is allowed to react. Every later toggle updates the store and flashes either the staying-logged-in or session-only message for 2.5 seconds. The Transition merely fades that notice. No authentication token is handled in this file, and the timeout has no unmount cleanup. The Magpie admires the clear ownership, though it would pocket the timer handle for tidier departure.

The treatment is structural, not behavioral. Two named slots occupy absolute front and back faces inside a perspective container. Both faces hide their reverse side; the back begins rotated 180 degrees, and the shared inner panel rotates to 180 degrees when the required `flipped` prop is true. A half-second transform transition supplies the turn. The outer container permits vertical overflow, but the Component owns no click, timer, local state, or emitted event. The parent decides when recovery becomes reversal and provides both faces. This file only guarantees the geometry of the change.

The future is exactly two-sided. The front and back named slots are laid into absolute faces with hidden backs; the rear face is pre-rotated, while the inner wrapper rotates when `flipped` becomes true. Perspective, preserved 3D transforms, and a 500-millisecond transition create the visual exchange. Nothing in the Component changes the prop, listens for interaction, or announces completion. Its prophecy is therefore limited and reliable: given two supplied faces and a Boolean controlled elsewhere, it will show one orientation or the other. Any richer state machine belongs to the parent.

The header renders every `editableIcon` through `icon-shell`, followed by the add-icon shell, inside a horizontally scrollable strip. Edge buttons appear only when more than 68 pixels remain in their direction. Pressing an edge starts smooth 45-pixel steps every 50 milliseconds until mouseup or mouseleave. Mouse and touch dragging directly adjust scrollLeft. Outside the strip, Smartbar editing exposes settings, optional revert when changes exist, and confirm. A ResizeObserver recomputes edge visibility and disconnects on unmount. One footnote: `scrollInterval` is stopped by pointer events but is not explicitly cleared in the unmount hook.
